story:
An old man sits across from us. Folds in his coat. Folds in his skin. A scarf tight around his throat. He speaks German. Curt. Brisk. Loud, as if volume is meaning. He points to his neck.

Elijah, three years old, pats my cheek. Cinnamon-cookie fingers. “He wants you to close the window, Grandma. He doesn’t want a sore froat.”

Elijah likes yogurt and eggs cooked hard. He doesn’t like food colors to touch. He pretends he’s a dragon. Or a knight. One at a time. Like his food. Singular joys.

“His froat, Grandma. His froat.” Sugar crystals glitter at the corner of his mouth. If he were my daughter, I’d wipe them. He is my grandson, so I don’t. His bottom lip disappears. Sticky lips. Crummy fingers. He can stand one, not the other; sticky hands would undo him. I don’t move. I’m thinking of the hand on my cheek attached to a little boy attached to my heart. I’m thinking of five weeks ago on this train, going the other way. The start. I’m thinking of now. The finish. My heart breaks under a tiny hand.

The train stops. Doors open. A boy and his bike get off. A woman and her baby get on. Doors close. Their worlds slide.

Before the next stop, my daughter, Danielle, leans over and shuts the window I opened. Heat and fresh August air cut off. The old man nods. Curt. Brisk. Hard. Danielle whispers, “They think drafts make you ill. The scarf keeps off the chill.” I want to say a breeze won’t make him sick. I can’t. Elijah’s Einstein hair brushes my nose. He smells like soap and play. The sweat of three is sweet. I’d bottle it if I could. He presses into me. He tugs a hair on my arm. I tug one on his leg. The train clicks to stop thirteen.

Silence. Flat fields unroll in green and yellow. Neat as patchwork, hemmed with trees. Ordered, like the people who planted them. I try to forget where I’m going. The train thumps. Faces change. The scarfed man reads a book. The train attendant reads the train. A mother gathers purse, suitcase, sleeping child, and slips away before the doors close.

Elijah rolls a toy car on my knee. Vroom, vroom. He stops. Thinks. “Can I come to Canada?” Blue eyes. Wide. Hopeful. Danielle and I look at each other. Crushed by his wanting. “It takes money, Elijah. We don’t have the money.” He knows money. It buys gelato in Landsberg. Schweinshaxe at the Metzgerwirt. A paddle boat on the Bodensee. He lowers his head. Puts the car away. Burrows into his mother. I ache.

Houses pass under red tile, sometimes black, sometimes gray. Another day I’d imagine the lives behind those windows. Not today. Today my throat burns. My chest pounds.

“Neunzehn,” the speaker says. “Nineteen, Grandma,” Elijah translates. Factories replace fields. Apartments replace houses. City smell creeps in. Dread threads the sliding doors. Tourists smile. Locals don’t. “They’re friendly in homes,” Danielle explains. “Not on streets, in shops, on trains.”

I list what I must carry—suitcases, passport, water, purse—but think only of hair like silk, fists with pudge, and home far from his everyday. Tears threaten.

He stands. Digs into his pocket. A brass-rimmed euro shines. Surprise surprises him. Joy floods. Even stern faces soften. “Mama! I have money!” Danielle and I cry. He doesn’t know why. She tells him it’s not enough for Canada. Joy drops from his face. We wipe tears and snot. The train slows. The announcement comes. I don’t know the words, but I know what they mean.

Next stop: Munich Airport. Exit on the left. Stop twenty-two. The doors open.
